shortDefinition: Saxenda is the brand name for liraglutide approved for chronic weight management, a daily GLP-1 injection that reduces appetite and supports metabolic health.
fullDefinition:
## What is Saxenda?

**Saxenda** is the brand name for **[liraglutide]** when prescribed for chronic weight management. It is a once-daily injectable **GLP-1 receptor agonist** approved by the FDA in 2014 — the first GLP-1 medication ever approved specifically for obesity. The same active ingredient is sold as **[victoza]** for type 2 diabetes, but at a lower maximum dose.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, Saxenda is one of several metabolic options for women whose weight is being driven by insulin resistance, perimenopause, hypothyroidism, or PCOS — situations where calories alone do not explain what the body is doing.

## How does Saxenda work?

Saxenda mimics the gut hormone **GLP-1**, which the body naturally releases after meals. By binding to GLP-1 receptors in the brain, gut, and pancreas, it:

- Slows stomach emptying so meals feel more satisfying
- Reduces appetite and food preoccupation
- Improves insulin response to meals
- Lowers glucagon between meals

Unlike weekly GLP-1 medications, Saxenda is taken every day, so steady-state levels are reached relatively quickly and side effects can be managed with smaller, more flexible dose adjustments.

## When is Saxenda prescribed?

Saxenda is FDA-approved for adults with:

- **BMI ≥ 30** (obesity), or
- **BMI ≥ 27** plus at least one weight-related condition such as hypertension, dyslipidemia, type 2 diabetes, or sleep apnea

It is also approved for adolescents 12-17 with obesity, under specialist care. In clinical trials, average weight loss was around 5-8% of body weight over a year — less than newer agents like semaglutide or tirzepatide, but meaningful for many patients, especially those who cannot access or tolerate weekly options.

## Patient considerations

The most common side effects are gastrointestinal — nausea, diarrhea, constipation, and reflux — particularly during the first few weeks of dose escalation. Saxenda carries the same boxed warning as other [glp-1-agonists] regarding medullary thyroid cancer and MEN-2 syndrome and should not be used in those patients. It is not used in pregnancy. Daily injections require commitment, and weight tends to creep back if the medication is stopped without a structured nutrition, strength, and sleep plan in place. At MTC we also recommend periodic [tsh] checks because rapid weight changes can shift thyroid hormone needs. Saxenda is a tool — a useful one — but it works best inside a thoughtful, individualized plan built with your clinician.

commonQuestions.a: For most patients, no — but it depends on the goal. Wegovy (semaglutide) is once-weekly and produces greater average weight loss in clinical trials, around 15% of body weight versus 5-8% with Saxenda. However, Saxenda's daily dosing can be an advantage for women who want more granular control over side effects, who prefer to ramp up more slowly, or who do not have access to Wegovy due to supply or insurance issues. Both work; the right choice depends on tolerance, access, and your goals — a clinician can help you weigh trade-offs.

commonQuestions.q: How long do I need to stay on Saxenda?
commonQuestions.a: Saxenda is approved for chronic — meaning long-term — use, because obesity is a chronic relapsing condition. Most patients regain a meaningful portion of weight within a year of stopping the medication, similar to other [glp-1-agonists]. At Modern Thyroid Clinic we view Saxenda as one tool inside a longer-term metabolic plan, alongside protein-forward nutrition, strength training, sleep, and thyroid optimization. Some women stay on it indefinitely; others taper successfully once durable habits are in place. The right duration is an individual decision made with your clinician.

commonQuestions.q: Can Saxenda help with hormonal weight gain in perimenopause?
commonQuestions.a: It can, particularly when the weight gain is driven by rising insulin resistance — a very common pattern in [perimenopause]. As estrogen fluctuates and falls, many women develop more visceral fat and stronger cravings, even with the same diet. Saxenda's effects on appetite, satiety, and insulin response often help break that cycle. It is most powerful when paired with strength training (to preserve muscle), adequate protein, sleep, and — when appropriate — hormone optimization. We commonly use it alongside [bioidentical-hormone-replacement-therapy] in our perimenopausal patients.

seoTitle: Saxenda (Liraglutide): Weight Loss Injection Uses & Considerations
seoDescription: Saxenda is daily liraglutide approved for chronic weight management. Learn how this GLP-1 medication works and who it helps most.
